description: California ballot proposition to classify app-based drivers as independent contractors rather than employees, exempting them from AB5. YES (keep independent) won 58.6% vs NO (make employees) 41.4%. Most expensive ballot measure in US history at $224M+. Domain: political/labor.

key_events.description: Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, Instacart, and Postmates commit $200M+ to YES campaign, making it the most expensive ballot measure in US history.
YES campaign saturates California media with ads featuring drivers saying they prefer flexibility. In-app messaging reaches millions of users.
COVID-19 pandemic increases public reliance on delivery apps, shifting framing from labor rights to essential service continuity.
Labor unions and NO campaign struggle to match spending. Message complexity (AB5 law, contractor vs employee distinction) disadvantages NO.
Late polls show YES pulling away. Undecided voters break toward YES amid fear of losing ride-hail/delivery services.

calibration_notes: Tests asymmetric spending effect and corporate narrative dominance. YES outspent NO roughly 10:1 ($200M vs $20M). Key dynamics: massive advertising saturation moving undecideds, framing flexibility vs exploitation, in-app messaging as novel voter contact channel, COVID context boosting gig economy legitimacy, complexity of issue favoring status quo framing (YES = keep things as they are), and corporate campaign sophistication vs grassroots labor opposition.
